coredata.dc:title: A sustainable synthesis and applications of biomass waste-derived tunable fluorescent carbon dots: In vitro and in vivo fluorescent imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Krishnaiah, Prakash
Atchudan, Raji
Gangadaran, Prakash
Perumal, Suguna
Rajendran, Ramya Lakshmi
Sundramoorthy, Ashok K.
Suresh Kumar, Raju
Ramalingam, Srinivasan
Ahn, Byeong-Cheol
Lee, Seung Woo
Lee, Yong Rok
coredata.dc:description: Different heteroatoms introduced to carbon dots (CDs) have been used extensively owing to their excellent optical properties, simple synthesis method, and multifunctional applications such as environmental detection, multicolor cell imaging, and gene therapy/delivery. Nitrogen and sulfur codoped carbon dots (PSNCDs) were prepared using persimmon fruit (Diospyros kaki) peel biomass waste and sodium thiosulphate by the straight route, showing excellent optical properties. The resulting PSNCDs exhibit consistent fluorescence emission at 440 nm under excitation at 360 nm, with a high quantum yield (approximately 16 %), making them suitable for a range of applications, including bioimaging, drug delivery, sensing, catalysts, and so on. The as-synthesized PSNCDs were subjected to various analytical methods to confirm their morphology and surface functionalization. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy displayed that the PSNCDs are predominately 2–4 nm with an average size of around 3 nm. Fourier-transform infrared and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis confirmed the amine and sulfur functional moieties on the outer surfaces and edges of the PSNCDs. The uniform particle size distribution and structural morphology were confirmed by transmission electron microscopy analysis. The Raman and X-ray diffraction investigation reveals that the as-synthesized PSNCDs appeared to be defective graphite-like structures with an intensity ratio of D to G is 0.65. Furthermore, PSNCDs were also utilized as fluorescent probes for cellular imaging. PSNCDs exhibited enhanced biocompatibility (cell viability 98 %) with regular fibroblast cells; in both in vitro and in vivo scenarios, they exhibited robust fluorescence signals upon fluorescent imaging of cultured fibroblast cells. These characteristics collectively validate the potential of PSNCDs for cell imaging without the need for supplementary modifications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Persimmon fruit
Carbon dots
Biocompatibility
Fibroblast cells
Cellular imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Multicolor in vitro imaging of cells using PSNCDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (NRF-2019R1I1A3A01063308 and NRF-2022R1I1A1A01068652). This work was partially supported by the Researchers Supporting Project Number ( RSP2024R142 ), King Saud University , Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation for the synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots.
XRD pattern (a) and Raman spectra (b) of as-synthesized PSNCDs.
TEM images (a–b), distribution graph of particle size (c), and FT-IR spectrum (d) of as-synthesized PSNCDs.
Narrow-scan XPS spectrum of carbon (a), nitrogen (b), oxygen (c), and sulfur (d) of as-synthesized PSNCDs.

(a) UV–visible absorbance (inset: optical images of aqueous PSNCDs under natural light (left) 365 nm UV light in the dark (right)), (b) fluorescence excitation and emission curves, (c) fluorescence emission curves at the variation of excitation wavelengths, and (d) the equivalent normalized excitation-dependent fluorescence emission curves of achieved PSNCDs. Fluorescence emission curves of PSNCDs (e) at different storage periods and (f) at different irradiation times.

PSNCDs on cell viability and multicolor imaging of 2D culture mouse ordinary fibroblast cells. (a) The cell viability (CCK8) assay outcomes of fibroblast cells treated with the synthesized PSNCDs (0, 2.5, 5, 7.5, 10, 12.5, 15, 7.5, 20, 22.5, and 25 µg/mL) for 24 h. The data is epitomized in bar chart form as percentages (n = 3). The student t-test was executed for statistical analysis. Not significant denoted as NS. (b) Laser confocal microscopy pictures of fibroblast cells incubated with 12.5 μg/mL of PSNCDs for 24 h. Note: The scale bar in the image is 20 μm.

In vivo fluorescent imaging of PSNCDs injected in mice. (a) In vivo fluorescent imaging of PSNCDs in mouse. PSNCDs (50 μg/mL) or PBS (control) were administered subcutaneously at the right flank. (b) Ex vivo fluorescent imaging of organs of PSNCDs or PBS (control) injected in mouse.
Comparison of optical properties of carbon dots with quantum yield from different precursors and different synthesis methods.

row.entry: In vitro and in vivo imaging

head.ce:title: A sustainable synthesis and applications of biomass waste-derived tunable fluorescent carbon dots: In vitro and in vivo fluorescent imaging

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Different heteroatoms introduced to carbon dots (CDs) have been used extensively owing to their excellent optical properties, simple synthesis method, and multifunctional applications such as environmental detection, multicolor cell imaging, and gene therapy/delivery. Nitrogen and sulfur codoped carbon dots (PSNCDs) were prepared using persimmon fruit (Diospyros kaki) peel biomass waste and sodium thiosulphate by the straight route, showing excellent optical properties. The resulting PSNCDs exhibit consistent fluorescence emission at 440 nm under excitation at 360 nm, with a high quantum yield (approximately 16 %), making them suitable for a range of applications, including bioimaging, drug delivery, sensing, catalysts, and so on. The as-synthesized PSNCDs were subjected to various analytical methods to confirm their morphology and surface functionalization. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy displayed that the PSNCDs are predominately 2–4 nm with an average size of around 3 nm. Fourier-transform infrared and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis confirmed the amine and sulfur functional moieties on the outer surfaces and edges of the PSNCDs. The uniform particle size distribution and structural morphology were confirmed by transmission electron microscopy analysis. The Raman and X-ray diffraction investigation reveals that the as-synthesized PSNCDs appeared to be defective graphite-like structures with an intensity ratio of D to G is 0.65. Furthermore, PSNCDs were also utilized as fluorescent probes for cellular imaging. PSNCDs exhibited enhanced biocompatibility (cell viability 98 %) with regular fibroblast cells; in both in vitro and in vivo scenarios, they exhibited robust fluorescence signals upon fluorescent imaging of cultured fibroblast cells. These characteristics collectively validate the potential of PSNCDs for cell imaging without the need for supplementary modifications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Disposal of waste biomass, particularly biomass generated from urban areas, is a big and complex issue [1]. Effective disposal methods and high-value biomass utilization are important for developing sustainable resources and addressing the current ecological issues triggered by biomass and its waste [2]. Among the biomass waste produced in urban areas around the world, disposal of food waste causes major environmental issues. This is due to the generation of a huge amount of food waste as compared to consuming it [3]. Persimmon fruit (Diospyros kaki) is commonly grown in South Korea as a seasonal fruit and is one of the most important and highly consumed fruits. Once the peel is removed, the fruit can be eaten raw or cooked, and the peel is generally discarded. Because of this removal of the persimmon fruit peel, a significant amount of organic waste/biowaste is generated. Utilization of these persimmon fruit peel waste to generate carbon-based nanomaterials is one of the sustainable approaches toward an eco-friendly environment [4]. All plant sources consist of polyphenols, carbohydrates, minerals, vitamin C, dietary fiber, chlorophylls, carotenoids, and antioxidants [5–7].

Carbon dots (CDs) are a class of new fluorescent zero-dimensional nanomaterials that are synthesized by an easy synthesis method and have exceptional properties such as outstanding optical properties, low cytotoxicity, and exceptional biocompatibility [8,9]. These CDs find a wide variety of applications, which include chemo-sensing, bioimaging, photo-thermal therapy, drug delivery, and so on [10–13]. It is a well-proven fact that the carbonization conditions (e.g., hydrothermal route, solvothermal method, microwave-assisted way, pyrolysis route, and electrochemical oxidation process) and various types of precursor materials, including small organic compounds, biomolecules, and natural products, affect the functionalities, physicochemical properties and photo-luminescent (PL) properties of CDs [14]. It is well-known that instinctive materials are rich in chemical components with plentiful necessary heteroatoms (e.g., nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P), and sulfur (S)), which are the perfect raw materials for the production of idiosyncratic biomass CDs with governable PL properties [15,16].

CDs have been designed for the detection of heavy metal ions, protein, DNA, dopamine, glutathione, hypochlorite, ascorbic acid, nitrogen dioxide (NO2) gas, and some heavy (hazardous) metal ions such as mercury (Hg2+), silver (Ag+), lead (Pb2+), and copper (Cu2+) [17–21]. Apart from these applications, CDs can also be extensively used for the application of counterfeiting [22]. Counterfeiting is a rising worldwide concern that businesses, administrations, and customers encounter. Anti-counterfeiting practices that bring genuine kinds of stuff that are difficult to duplicate and easier to validate are, therefore vital for the protection of brands and important documents such as bank notes, diplomas, and certificates [23]. In recent times, nanoparticles such as CDs with high luminescent properties have found great potential for anti-counterfeiting applications with a wide range of security applications, such as numerous kinds of security inks [24].

Most importantly, CDs are frequently utilized in the application of biological/biomedical fields, including bioimaging (in vitro and in vivo), tissue therapy, and targeted drug delivery. In vitro and in vivo imaging are two distinct techniques widely used in medical and biological research to visualize and study various biological processes [25,26]. In vitro imaging involves examining cells, tissues, or biological samples outside of their natural environment, typically in laboratory settings. This technique allows researchers to investigate cellular (living/dead cells) or subcellular structures and functions through microscopy [27,28]. On the other hand, in vivo imaging involves imaging the structures and functions within their natural environment. It enables the non-invasive visualization and monitoring of cellular or subcellular biological processes [29–31]. The term 'in vivo' is derived from Latin, meaning 'within the living,' emphasizing the focus on examining biological phenomena within their living and dynamic setting. The fluorescent proteins (GFP, Red fluorescent protein, mCherry, tdTomato) are unsuitable for long-term in vivo imaging purposes due to their low photostability and weak fluorescence signal [32]. This limitation requires exploring alternative fluorophores with better properties for prolonged imaging. Therefore, developing more efficient fluorescent agents is essential for extended in vivo cellular imaging.

In this investigation, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs (PSNCDs) were synthesized from the waste biomass of persimmon fruit peel using a simple hydrothermal method. The persimmon fruits are a popular fruit consumed worldwide. Thus, here, we have consumed the persimmon fruit peel as a carbon source for the synthesis of durable fluorescence PSNCDs. The main aim of this work is to focus on the possible reuse of biowaste/food waste to reduce the growing global biowaste that impacts environmental issues. Various bio-sources have already been used to prepare CDs. The surface functional moieties, size, and morphology of as-synthesized fluorescent PSNCDs were confirmed by various analytical methods such as Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR), X-ray Diffraction (XRD), Raman, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The characterizations confirmed that PSNCDs are rich in nitrogen (amine), carbonyl, and hydroxyl groups with an acceptable quantum yield of 16 %. The biocompatibility of PSNCDs was analyzed with normal fibroblast cells and applied for fluorescence imaging cultured cells both in vitro and in vivo without further modifications. To the best of our knowledge, this is the earliest report on in vivo cellular imaging (animal study) using the waste of persimmon fruit feel-derived PSNCDs with good biocompatibility.

A simple one-step hydrothermal treatment was adopted to prepare the PSNCDs. Firstly, a well-dried persimmon fruit peel waste biomass was ground well. Then, 0.5 g of grounded persimmon fruit peel waste biomass powder and 0.5 mg of sodium thiosulphate were placed into a 100 mL glass beaker containing 50 mL double distilled water. Then, the solution was subjected to ultrasonication for 30 min. Then, the well-dispersed solution mixture was directly placed into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel commercial autoclave without any filtration. The solution-filled autoclave was securely closed, positioned in a commercially available hot air oven, and subsequently heated at 180 °C for 36 h. Then, the resulting primitive material was filtered through a membrane of 0.22 μm (pore diameter) to remove the bigger materials. Finally, the clear PSNCD aqueous dispersion was collected and stored in a commercially available refrigerator. The final PSNCD aqueous solution was dried in freeze-drying in order to get solid materials for further characterization. The detailed synthesis procedure of PSNCDs is schematically exemplified in Fig. 1 .

The luminous PSNCDs were prepared from persimmon fruit peel biomass leftover via a simple hydrothermal route, and the preparation method is shown in Fig. 1. The XRD pattern of as-prepared PSNCDs (Fig. 2
(a)) reveals the peaks positioned at 2θ = 24 and 44°, which are associated with the (002) and (100) planes of carbon-like nanomaterials, respectively [33]. The XRD pattern of the PSNCDs closely matches the reported JCPDS card number (JCPDS Card No. 41–1487) of typical graphitic carbon (graphite/graphene). The PSNCDs have a largely graphitic-like structure with an interlayer arrangement distance of 0.37 nm, which is ascribed to the (002) plane, and 0.21 nm is credited to the (100) plane. The arrangement of d-spacing is greater than the interlayer arrangement distance between the (002) planes in typical bulk graphite (∼0.34 nm), owing to the existence of numerous natural functional (oxygen-containing) moieties and or amorphous (defective/ disordered) carbon segments [34]. The computed d-spacing of 0.21 nm is associated with the plane (100) facets of carbon-related materials. In addition, the wider peaks were seen in the XRD graph, corroborating the very minor particles and or amorphous (surface defective/disordered) materials. The functionalities on the surface/edges of the PSNCDs are the outcome of the slight amorphous region.

Besides, the graphitization/crystallization of the prepared PSNCDs was observed by the well-known Raman spectroscopy, and the associated Raman spectrum is depicted in Fig. 2 (b). Raman spectrum of as-prepared PSNCDs displayed two wider peaks at the position of 1395 and 1590 cm−1 [35]. The peak 1395 cm−1 is conservatively denoted as the D-band, which is identified to become flawed/disordered crystallites of graphite-like structure. The high-frequency peak positioned at 1590 cm−1 is conventionally stated as the G-band of graphite-like structure and is assigned to the C=C in-plane stretching vibration (E2g) of the graphite-like models [36]. Hence, the D to G band intensity ratio (ID/IG) denotes the concentration of shortcomings in the model of the graphitic structure, and the low value of ID/IG in PSNCDs suggests the integrity of the graphitic shells is adequately high to shield the core carbon material well from corrosion and surface oxidation. The computed ID/IG ratio of the PSNCDs is 0.65, which exposes that the PSNCDs have a suitable degree of graphitization. Influence from surface/edge functional groups (moieties) and/or the amorphous nature of carbon, while the preparation cannot be omitted, leads to a broad disordered peak (D-band).

The surface/structural morphology and size distribution of as-prepared PSNCDs were examined using the TEM technique. The TEM images of PSNCDs are represented in Fig. 3
(a and b), which clearly shows that the PSNCDs are spherical and monodispersed. The magnified TEM image in inset Fig. 3 (a) shows that the lattice spacing of PSNCDs is 0.21 nm associated with the (100) facet of graphite, which confirms that the PSNCDs comprise similar to that of graphite structures [37,38]. Fig. 3 (c) represents the size distribution of PSNCDs ranging from 1.5 to 5.0 nm, with a computed mean diameter of 3.0 nm. These results are in good agreement with the XRD and Raman spectroscopy results.

The chemical composition of PSNCDs was distinguished by FT-IR. As exposed in Fig. 3 (d), the wider band is ascribed to the stretching vibrations mode of hydroxyl (O–H) and amine-related (N–H) groups in the wavelength span of 3200–3400 cm−1 [39]. The C–H symmetric stretching vibration is detected in the range of 2847 cm−1, and asymmetric stretching vibration and 2926 cm−1 [40]. The distinguish absorption peaks situated at 1764, 1590, 1402, 1175, 1105, and 1048 cm−1 were identified as vibrational absorption bands connected with C=O (carbonyl/carboxyl groups), C=C (graphitic sp2-hybridization), C–N/SO2 (amino/sulfoxide groups), C–O, C=S, and C–O–C chemical bonds, respectively [41,42]. The sharp peak at 615 cm−1 is assigned to functionalities of C–S/C–H bonds. Such outcomes confirmed that N and S elements were effectively functionalized in the structural framework of PSNCDs [41]. In addition to this, the determination of the elemental composition and edges/outer surface functional moieties of the PSNCDs and the XPS analytical measurement were recorded, and related results were discussed in depth. Wide-scan spectra ( Figure S1 ) displayed three distinguish peaks at the energy ranges of 285 eV (C 1 s), 399 eV (N 1 s), 531 eV (O 1 s), and a weak peak at 164 eV (S 2p). Apart from these peaks, the sodium (Na 1 s) peaks appear at 1071 eV. The sodium originated from the source of sodium thiosulphate. Remarkably, the atomic ratio of carbon to other functionalities was approximately 1:1.5, and the atomic percentages of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, sulfur, and sodium were 43.0, 32.0, 5.0, 1.0, and 19.0 %, respectively. The narrow-scan spectrum of Na 1 s peaks centered at 1071 eV, as shown in Figure S2 . As the XRD does not reveal the presence of sodium, it is believed that the sodium might not be coordinated with the carbon framework; instead, it is present as a sodium derivative form in the PSNCDs [43]. The narrow-scan spectrum of C 1 s presents four peaks with binding energy levels at 284.2, 284.8, 285.9, and 288.2 eV (Fig. 4
a). These peaks were attributed to –CH2/CH3 (out plane), C–C/C=C (sp3-/sp2-hybridization), C–S/C–N/C–S (N and S associated with the C in the structural framework of PSNCDs), C=N/C=O (O-related functional moieties connected with the C in the structural skeleton of PSNCDs)/C=S bonds, respectively [44]. The N 1 s narrow-scan XPS spectrum (Fig. 4 b) is divided into two peaks at 397.3 and 398.9 eV, related to the pyridine-like nitrogen and nitrogen atoms in the graphitic-nitrogen structure (N–C3)/amine-like functional moieties (N–H bonds), respectively. The O 1 s narrow-scan XPS spectrum (Fig. 4 c) exhibited three peaks at 530.0, 531.0, and 535.4 eV, linked to C=O (carbonyl group), C–OH (hydroxyl moieties)/C–O–C (epoxy moieties)/O=C–OH (carboxyl moieties), and H–O–H (physically adsorbed water molecules, adsorption due to excellent hydrophilic nature of PSNCDs), respectively [45]. For the S 2p narrow-scan XPS spectrum (Fig. 4 d), the peaks at energy levels of 160.3, 162.9, 167.4, and 168.9 eV pointing to four forms of sulfur moieties including C–S (S 2p3/2), C–S (S 2p1/2), sulfoxides-related S and sulfate-related S. These attained results were in harmony with that of the achieved FT-IR spectral result, which implies the manifestation of heteroatoms like N and S moieties in PSNCDs edges/outer surface structure.

The ultraviolet (UV)-visible absorption of the hydrothermally synthesized PSNCDs (Fig. 5
(a)) displays in the UV region with two broad peaks found in the range of 250–400 nm, which are attributed to the π–π* transition of the conjugated graphitic (C=C, sp2-hybridization) bonds (∼275 nm) and n–π* transition of the carbonyl/carboxyl (C=O) bonds (∼325 nm) and an end extending into the region of visible range. These bonds are similar to previously reported carbon dot-based materials [46,47]. Inset Fig. 5 (a) displays the aqueous dispersion of PSNCDs under daylight (left) and 4 W energy of 365 nm UV light (right). The aqueous dispersion of PSNCDs exhibited a cyan-blue color when kept under UV light (365 nm), but it was a pale yellow color when kept under daylight.

The excitation and emission spectra of the synthesized PSNCDs are shown in Fig. 5 (b). The highest excitation and emission intensity reached around 360 and 440 nm, respectively. To further examine the quantum yield of PSNCDs, the quantum yield of PSNCDs was measured using quinine sulfate as a standard substance. The computed quantum yields for PSNCDs are 16 %. The resulting quantum yield designates that the synthesized PSNCDs exhibit high luminescent/fluorescent abilities, surpassing and or comparable to many other CDs. Excitation wavelength-dependent emission is one of the distinguished characteristics of synthesized PSNCDs. The fluorescence spectra of PSNCDs with varying excitation wavelengths from 290 to 450 nm are shown in Fig. 5 (c). The fluorescence emission intensity of the PSNCDs gradually increases with an increase in the excitation wavelength up to 360 nm. Later, the fluorescence emission intensity of the PSNCDs slowly goes down while increasing the excitation wavelength beyond 360 nm. A strong fluorescence emission peak of PSNCDs was located at 440 nm with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The fluorescence emission intensity is dependent on the excitation wavelength. The smaller PSNCD particles get excited at lower wavelengths, and bigger particles get excited at higher wavelengths [48,49]. The fluorescence emission intensity depends on the quantity (number) of nanoparticles excited at a specific wavelength. The maximum fluorescence emission intensity of PSNCDs was distinguished at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, which might be due to the most significant number of nanoparticles being excited at that wavelength. At the same time, the fluorescence emission peaks were shifted to the higher wavelengths with the increasing excitation wavelengths. The red shift is undoubtedly noticeable when the fluorescence emission intensity is normalized at the variation of the excitation wavelengths, which is displayed in Fig. 5 (d). The fluorescence emission wavelength is correspondingly dependent on the wavelength of excitation. Moreover, the fluorescence spectral results demonstrated that the synthesized PSNCDs have excitation-dependent emission behavior. The fluorescence mechanism of PSNCDs is complicated. The excitation-dependent emission behavior is possibly a different size and variation of the surface energy traps of the PSNCDs [47]. Also, the excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior of PSNCDs is influenced by the nature of their edges/surfaces. The existence of different functional moieties on the outer surface/edges of the PSNCDs might have resulted in a sequence of emissive traps between π and π* transition of C–C (sp3-hybridization)/C=C (sp2-hybridization). A surface energy trap leads the emission by illuminating the CDs at a specific excitation wavelength. As the excitation wavelength changes, other equivalent surface/edge state emissive traps lead. Hence, the PL mechanism is presiding over by both the effects of particle size and defects (disorders) of the surface/edges [47].

The stability of fluorescent PSNCDs is one of the essential factors to assess for their practical applications [50]. The synthesized PSNCDs were stored for a long time, and the fluorescence behavior was measured and compared with PSNCDs when synthesizing time to determine their prolonged stability. Fluorescence emission spectra of PSNCDs at different storage periods are shown in Fig. 5 (e). The fluorescence intensity showed no apparent decrement even after long storage (120 days), and this result demonstrates that the PSNCDs are highly stable after long-term storage. Further, optical images under UV (365 nm) exposure have been taken at different storage times, as shown in Fig. S3 (a). No apparent variation was observed in the fluorescence brightness under UV light. Hence, the photographic images demonstrated that the PSNCDs are stable for a long time without any accumulation/coagulation or chemical reaction in the aqueous medium. The synthesized PSNCDs were exposed through UV light (365 nm) irradiation for various time spans to demonstrate their photostability. The fluorescence spectra of the fluorescent PSNCDs irradiated at different times are given in Fig. 5 (f). The fluorescence intensity of as-synthesized PSNCDs is an insignificant difference from the initial intensity even after 120 min of irradiation, which reveals the excellent photostability of PSNCDs [51]. Then, optical images under UV (365 nm) exposure were taken at different irradiation times, as shown in Fig. S3 (b). These images displayed that the PSNCD solution retained the fluorescence brightness even after 120 min of irradiation under UV light, suggesting its durability is excellent for long-term irradiation. Therefore, the synthesized PSNCDs appear to be good candidates for bioimaging applications [52]. In order to show the superiority of the current materials, the optical properties of the PSNCDs were compared with the other related materials reported in the past literature. Table 1 shows the comparison of optical properties for different CDs with various carbon precursors. The optical properties of the synthesized PSNCDs are auspiciously higher and comparable to the earlier reported CDs [16,47,53–65].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multicolor in vitro imaging of cells using PSNCDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Cell viability (CCK-8) assays divulged that the freshly produced PSNCDs have outstanding biocompatibility. In the existence of increasing concentrations of PSNCDs, the cultured mouse normal fibroblast cells (isolated from subcutaneous connective tissue, Adipose, and Areolar) maintained their viability after being cultured for 24 h at 37 °C. Cell viability was 97.55 %, even at the PSNCD concentration of 25 μg/mL, demonstrating admirable biocompatibility (Fig. 6
a). Upon examining the confocal laser scanning microscope images (Fig. 6 b), it was observed that cultured mouse ordinary fibroblast cells, which were incubated with PSNCDs for 24 h at 37 °C, exhibited the fluorescent indication within the cells. The signal displayed multiple colors (blue signal, green signal, and red signal), indicating successful cellular uptake of the PSNCDs. This multifaceted color spectrum points towards the comprehensive integration of the PSNCDs within the cellular environment. Moreover, the localization of these signals are throughout the cells, spanning both the cytoplasm and nucleus, provides compelling evidence that the PSNCDs have effectively penetrated the normal fibroblast cells of mice. The presence of fluorescent signals in the nucleus suggests a profound level of cellular internalization, further emphasizing the potential of PSNCDs as effective carriers or imaging agents within the cellular milieu, where efficient internalization is often a critical factor for efficacy [66]. No fluorescence signals were detected in cells that were not exposed to PSNCDs. These findings align with previous studies [58,67,68] and demonstrate that PSNCDs emit multiple colors in comparison to other fluorescent labeling agents. Consequently, the success of this cellular uptake process enhances the potential applications of PSNCDs in various biomedical fields, including targeted drug delivery and cellular imaging.

The fluorescence imaging conducted immediately after the injection of PSNCDs revealed robust signals in the region of the injections, specifically under the skin. However, these signals exhibited a noticeable reduction at 1 and 24 h post-injection, and by the 48-hour mark, no signals were observable. In contrast, the control mouse injected with PBS did not display any signals in the injection region, as depicted in Fig. 7
(a). Fluorescent imaging of ex vivo organs showed no signals, which indicates the clearance of PSNCDs from the mouse (Fig. 7b). This is an indication of a low/no accumulation level of the PSNCDs. This absence of signals in the organs aligns with findings from a previous study [69], indicating a low or negligible accumulation level of the PSNCDs. The ability to observe strong signals immediately after injection, followed by a noticeable reduction over time and eventual clearance from the organism, suggests a dynamic interaction and response to the PSNCDs within the biological system. The outcomes of this optical imaging in vivo are promising, underscoring its potential significance for advancing and exploring various biomedical applications.

In summary, a simple hydrothermal process was adopted to produce strong fluorescent PSNCDs from environmentally sustainable persimmon fruit peel biomass waste. Subsequent physicochemical characterization displayed that the obtained PSNCDs were successfully nitrogen/sulfur co-doped and exhibited excellent water dispersibility. The produced PSNCDs exhibited a spherical structure (average diameter ∼3.0 nm) with uniform particle size distribution, which was supported by TEM. Suface-defected graphitic carbon shells of PSNCDs were supported with XRD and Raman results. The FTIR spectrum evidenced the existence of –COOH, –OH, and –NH-related functional moieties on the outer surfaces/edges of PSNCDs, which was further confirmed by the XPS results. Furthermore, these PSNCDs revealed an acceptable quantum yield of 16 % without any chemical modification and or passivations. The cell cytotoxicity assessment of PSNCDs was conducted on a mouse with normal fibroblast cells and the live mice. The PSNCDs exhibited excellent optical stability over long irradiation times and good biocompatibility even at high concentrations, thus minimizing potential damage to cells during imaging. The mouse normal fibroblast cells exhibited remarkable compatibility with PSNCDs, demonstrating exceptional multicolor fluorescence emission and proving valuable for cell labeling purposes. Furthermore, the PSNCDs were successfully visualized in vivo, indicating their potential application as a versatile imaging tool for both in vitro (cell-based) and in vivo studies. The results of the current study not only contribute to the understanding of the behavior of PSNCDs in vivo but also hold great promise for the optimization of their properties for specific biomedical purposes. This exploration of PSNCDs in biomedical applications may lead to advancements in imaging techniques, drug delivery systems, and other therapeutic modalities, ultimately contributing to the progress of medical science and patient care.

Prakash Krishnaiah: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. Raji Atchudan: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Prakash Gangadaran: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. Suguna Perumal: Writing – review & editing. Ramya Lakshmi Rajendran: Visualization. Ashok K. Sundramoorthy: Visualization, Validation. Raju Suresh Kumar: Project administration. Srinivasan Ramalingam: Formal analysis. Byeong-Cheol Ahn: Supervision. Seung Woo Lee: Supervision, Validation. Yong Rok Lee: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by theMinistry of Education(NRF-2019R1I1A3A01063308 and NRF-2022R1I1A1A01068652). This work was also supported by the Researchers Supporting Project Number (RSP2024R142), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2024.115944.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sustainable synthesis of bright fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots from terminalia chebula for in vitro imaging

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots derived from Phyllanthus acidus utilized as a fluorescent probe for label-free selective detection of Fe(3+) ions, live cell imaging and fluorescent ink

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots for optical imaging in vivo
